Hari nails it:

“Don’t listen to people who don’t know how private markets work.”

Introducing USVC - a single basket of high-growth venture capital, for everyone. 

No accreditation required, SEC-registered, and a very low $500 minimum. 

Includes OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, Sierra, Crusoe, Legora, and Vercel. As USVC adds more companies, investors will own a piece of that too. 

Liquidity typically comes when companies exit, but we’re aiming to let investors redeem up to 5% of the fund every quarter. This isn’t guaranteed, but if we can make it work, you won’t be locked up like in a traditional venture fund.

It runs on AngelList, which already supports $125 billion of investor capital. 

And I’ve joined USVC as the Chairman of its Investment Committee.
—

Go back to the 1500s, you set sail for the new world to find tons of gold - that was adventure capital. 

Early-stage technology is the modern version. It says we are going to create something new, and it’s risky. It’s daring.

But ordinary people can’t invest until it’s old, until it’s no longer interesting, until everybody has access to it. By the time a stock IPOs, most of the alpha is gone. The adventure is gone. Public market investors are literally last in line.

This problem has become farcical in the last decade. Startups are reaching trillion dollar valuations in the private markets while ordinary investors have their noses up to the glass, wondering when they’ll be let in.

Investing in private markets isn’t easy. You need feet on the ground. You need judgment built over years. Most people don’t have the patience to wait ten or twenty years for an investment to come to fruition.

But there is no more productive, harder-working way to deploy a dollar than in true venture capital.

USVC enables you to invest in venture capital in a broad, accessible, professionally-managed way, through a single basket of innovation, focused on high-growth startups, at all stages. 

It is how you bet on the future of tech: the smartest young people in the world, working insane hours, leveraged to the max, with code, hardware, capital, media, and community. Your dollar doesn’t work harder anywhere.

There is an old line - in the future, either you are telling a computer what to do, or a computer is telling you what to do. You don’t want to be on the wrong side of that transaction.

USVC lets you buy the future, but you buy it now. Then you wait, and if you are right, you get paid.

Get access here:

https://t.co/pAj1sqUsG0
Truth, love, and beauty are all pursued for their own sake, even if they make us worse off.

dwarkesh_sp.text:
Ancient people never decided to adopt farming.

Agricultural practices gradually emerged out of a compounding series of small decisions, and they spread less by choice than by cultural competition.

This major transition in human history was decided by nobody. https://t.co/uT4AOlHDPo

There are so many counterintuitive facts in human genetics, talking to David Reich was just surprise after surprise.

"There's many places in your DNA where you're more closely related to a Neanderthal on your mother's side than you are to your father." https://t.co/PB93RSAlZd

David Reich calls it "an outstanding mystery of human history":

All of the genetic ingredients that are present in modern humans have been there for ~50,000 years, maybe even longer. So why did it take us so long to develop farming? https://t.co/KJUZjjOX6b

Between 100,000 and 50,000 years ago, human culture and technology exploded.

You might expect that this points to a major step-change in human evolution.

But David Reich and colleagues looked at the genetic data, and found no significant sweeps in this period at all. https://t.co/MPPQ2yxWAD

David Reich doesn't think population size has been a big constraint on human evolution. Why?

(1) The human genome already contains more than enough diversity for natural selection - we generally don't need to worry about having a large enough population for new mutations.

(2) Sometimes if selection is weak enough, it can be swamped by random genetic drift in small populations. But then it would be so weak that, even in a large population, it wouldn't make a difference on the timescales of human history. So population doesn't really matter either way.

Hunter-gatherers faced food instability on a short time scale: they would gorge themselves after a hunt, then might go days without any meat.

So they have an evolutionary advantage to keeping a higher body fat percentage - it keeps them going after a hunt.

Agricultural societies don't have this kind of day-to-day food insecurity. Farmers worry about food shortages on the scale of years, with disease and famine; marginally higher body fat wouldn't help with that.

So when a population switches to agriculture, natural selection begins to operate in the opposite direction, reducing body fat.

There's a common idea that, as human society grew more complex, individuals could specialise and outsource much of their thinking to the culture at large.

On this view, intelligence should have been maxed out in the hunter-gatherer period. Once complex societies took over, selection for individual intelligence would weaken.

But David Reich's lab finds the opposite pattern in the genome. The period when selection most aggressively favoured intelligence-linked genes was the Bronze Age, right as humans were organising themselves into city-states and empires.

What was the most important transition in human history, the thing that most drastically altered our species' way of life? David Reich's lab has found evidence pointing to a new answer.

There are two standard candidates:
1. The shift from hunting and gathering to farming, around 10,000 BC.
2. The Industrial Revolution, around 1800 AD.

Here's one way to adjudicate this:

When a species' environment changes drastically, natural selection accelerates, because the species has to catch up to adapt.

So the biggest transition will have happened in the period with the most rapid natural selection.

What David's lab has found is that the period with fastest natural selection wasn't 12000 years ago, and it wasn't our modern period.

It was the Bronze Age, about 5,000 to 2,000 years ago.

What was it about the Bronze Age that changed humans so profoundly?

David Reich is back.

He and collaborator Ali Akbari just published a paper that overturns a long-standing consensus about human evolution — that natural selection has been dormant in our species since the agricultural revolution.

By scaling ancient DNA sequencing and developing a new statistical method, they found that selection has actually sped up.

Selection went especially bonkers during the Bronze Age (around 3,000 years ago).

That's when gene frequencies for everything from immune function to body fat to intelligence were most in flux.

Over the last 10,000 years, selection pushed the genetic predictor of cognitive performance up by roughly a full standard deviation — most of it between 4,000 and 2,000 years ago.

After we finished recording, David sketched out on a whiteboard his new heretical model about who the Neanderthals really were. Luckily, I took out my iPhone and managed to record it.

He thinks the standard story (that Neanderthals are some separate archaic lineage we interbred with a little) just doesn't fit the evidence. Instead, he proposes that Neanderthals are essentially genetically-swamped modern humans.

A small population somewhere around the Caucasus invented Middle Stone Age technology roughly 300,000 years ago and expanded outward. The ones that moved into Europe interbred with local archaic humans, got genetically swamped, and became Neanderthals. The same expansion went into Africa, met much more diverged archaic Africans, and that mixture became us.

This means Neanderthals and modern humans share the same cultural ancestry — the only difference is which archaic humans they mixed with afterward.

David is a brilliant and rigorous scholar. It was a real delight to learn from him again.

0:00:00 – Ancient DNA suggests strong selection over last 10,000 years
0:16:24 – Natural selection intensified during the Bronze Age
0:35:40 – Why didn't evolution max out intelligence?
0:58:00 – Evolution is limited by time, not population size
1:09:40 – Why no farming before the Ice Age?
1:17:52 – The Neanderthal puzzle David can’t stop thinking about
1:54:40 – The methodology behind this breakthrough

It's very interesting that cryptographic protocols and neural networks have the same high-level architecture (where they jumble information as it moves sequentially across many layers). 

This is the result of a convergent evolution - cryptographic protocols need every output bit to depend on every input bit in complicated ways, and similarly, NNs need output to make connections between inputs.

But they're in some sense doing opposite things. While cryptographic protocols take something which has a lot of structure and make it seem indistinguishable from random, NNs take something which may look random and extract structure from it.

Much more on this idea in the full episode with @reinerpope

About 5000 years ago, horse nomads from the steppes conquered Europe. Still today, almost every European language descends from the one they spoke, and as much as 90% of European DNA comes from them.

What did this conquest actually look like on the ground? https://t.co/V1Rf1STChv

Pre-industrial populations weren't just shorter and less healthy - they were less intelligent, too.

A combination of fewer calories and a much higher burden of infectious disease meant that people's brains just developed less during childhood. https://t.co/vliWZwVcyV

AI training faces a very different set of trade-offs compared to human evolution. Because you can directly copy a trained model basically for free, it makes sense to amortize in the pre-trained weights the learning that humans spread across a lifetime. https://t.co/xV6zV8AxoA

David Reich on how much ancient DNA evidence has overturned so much consensus thinking how ancient cultures spread.

"It wasn't peaceful, it wasn't friendly, it wasn't nice.

Some of our archaeologist co-authors were just really distressed." https://t.co/JRuOeWDIuk

thejustinwelsh.text: RT @thejustinwelsh: A raise is a pay cut when it costs you the time to enjoy it.

An observation from building an 8-figure business over 7 years:

The best businesses are simple. Rookies tend to overcomplicate everything because it feels like progress. One avatar. One offer. One channel. Don't overcomplicate it until you get to $1M+ in profit per year.
A raise is a pay cut when it costs you the time to enjoy it.
RT @thejustinwelsh: The most successful people I know are annoyingly unaware of what everyone else is doing.
RT @thejustinwelsh: Marry well, stay in shape, build something you own, and protect your time.
The most successful people I know are annoyingly unaware of what everyone else is doing.
Marry well, stay in shape, build something you own, and protect your time.

A simple life hack: 

Make decisions that the status quo would call unreasonable. Leave stable jobs you hate. Say no to that promotion you don't want. Walk away from money that comes with strings attached. 

Great lives are built on unreasonable choices, not common ones.

Life hack 101:

Become completely addicted to improving yourself.

Ambition is useful when it's pointed at something you want.

If it isn't, you'll spend decades chasing, growing, and outpacing.

You'll be busy, and you may even look successful, but you'll never actually win.

The risk of quitting a job you hate is financial. 

The risk of staying in a job you hate is personal.
Past a certain point, money stops buying things you want and starts buying the absence of things you don't.
My life's goal is to make an impact while defending my family, hobbies, and health.

Three questions worth asking once a quarter:

1. What am I doing because I want to?

2. What am I doing because I think I'm supposed to?

3. What would I stop doing tomorrow if nobody was watching?

Some tradeoffs to be honest about:

-More money usually means less time
-More status usually means less freedom
-More ambition usually means less peace

None of them are wrong to choose. 

They're just wrong to choose by accident.

Every financial decision you make is either supporting your future or killing it.

Constantly upgrading your lifestyle kills it. Investing what you make supports it.

Most people don't think long-term, and end up at 50 with a lot of possessions, no money, and little time to fix it.

The people I admire all have a moment where they did something that looked unreasonable to the average person.

Took a pay cut for a better gig. Moved somewhere totally random. Started something everyone laughed at.

Most of us think of it as bravery, but it’s really conviction.

How to increase your luck:

- Read more
- Write more
- Build more
- Meet more people
- Introduce more people

Luck often comes from putting in more reps.
A weird side effect of building a simple business is that people always assume you're hiding the complicated part.
Nothing radicalizes you faster than getting paid while you were asleep for something you built when you were awake.

dickiebush.text:
A 6-step formula that will guarantee you failure:

1. Expect results to be instant
2. Start at an unsustainable pace
3. Think you know more than you do 
4. Switch between projects constantly
5. Give up the second you meet resistance
6. Compare yourself to those way ahead of you

RT @dickiebush: This is the most underrated productivity tool (and it costs $0):… https://t.co/WXDFzBFlh4 https://t.co/nxrEoy2GVL
You are doing way too much to stay in the same place and not nearly enough to grow
If you're using AI as a business owner, then you're already one step ahead

This is the most underrated productivity tool (and it costs $0):                                                                                                                                                                                                                        

Daily walks in nature.

Since my mid-20s, I've been a step maxxer.

15-20k steps per day MINIMUM.

Walking should become your default activity if you are not actively producing something.

It's elite for your:

• Endorphins
• Body composition
• Creative thinking

The second I feel stagnant or low, I instantly drop everything and start walking. 

I could talk for hours about the positive impact taking an absurd number of daily steps has had on my life.

WALK MORE

On a walk is when 95% of my writing happens:

• Thinking
• Outlining
• Observing
• Brainstorming

The last 5% (when I actually sit down to type) comes later.
RT @dickiebush: How to use ChatGPT to generate 100 ideas to write about in only 5 minutes—even if you think you have nothing to say: https:…
Working on a timer is an easy hack to realize just how little time you actually spend working
It’s amazing what you can accomplish once you opt out of status games

Weekly reminder:

The internet lets you be niche at scale (and rewards you heavily for it)

I can quickly name a handful of strategic mistakes I’ve made in the last year trying to scale my business:                                                                                                                                                                            

- Over-hiring / adding excess capacity too quickly in an effort to scale

- Spending money on unprofitable ad campaigns rather than doubling down on what was working organically

- Running a repeated event too many times rather than coming up with completely new material

- Outsourcing our content production too much rather than continuing to write new things

- Rolling out an internal or external policy change without thinking through literally every pushback scenario

- And on and on

But rather than look at these as “negative” mistakes, I’ve reframed them as a positive.

Because now, I think of failure as getting paid “Mistake Dividends.”

Let me explain:

If you think about making an investment in something, a new initiative, channel, business, team, etc. you can think about it as buying an asset.

And when you buy an asset, two things can happen:

- It can work out. If so, great, you made a good strategic choice

- If it doesn’t work out, you still collect an asset in the form of a mistake

Obviously, the first has a clear positive return.

And if you allow it to, the second scenario, despite being “failure,” can pay you back a positive dividend in multiple ways:

- You can avoid making that mistake again in the future (saving you future time/money)

- You can tell the story of that mistake to other people (like I’m doing now), so hopefully they can avoid it too

- And in the future, you can laugh at how inexperienced or naive you were at various stages of your business journey

All three of these are net positives in the long run. And you only get them if you try something uncertain and it doesn’t work out.

So this reframe helps me take on things with fixed downside, knowing they will pay me in the future one way or another.
Writing strengthens this muscle btw

Your monthly reminder to ban this list of words:

1. just
2. that
3. very
4. really
5. literally
6. actually
7. certainly
8. probably
9. basically

Ruthlessly edit these out of your writing.
AI makes it possible for you to write anything you want. The problem now is most people have no clue what they actually want.
Insane how much I still learn about the art of writing from Cole after nearly 6 years
